title: Getting Started with roptuna
text: roptuna R port Python Optuna framework (Akiba et al. 2019). automates hyperparameter search using define--run API: instead defining search space upfront, call trial$suggest_*() inside objective function, sampler adapts based worked .

title: Getting Started with roptuna
text: trial object passed objective exposes methods: trial$suggest_float(name, low, high, log = FALSE) — continuous parameter trial$suggest_int(name, low, high) — integer parameter trial$suggest_categorical(name, choices) — categorical parameter trial$suggest_log_uniform(name, low, high) — log-uniform continuous Parameters named idempotent: calling suggest_float("lr", 0, 1) twice trial always returns value.

title: Getting Started with roptuna
code: library(roptuna)  objective <- function(trial) {   x  <- trial$suggest_float("x", -5, 5)   y  <- trial$suggest_int("y", 1L, 5L)   x^2 + y }  study <- create_study(direction = "minimize", sampler = tpe_sampler(seed = 42)) study$optimize(objective, n_trials = 50)  cat("Best value:", study$best_value, "\n") cat("Best params:", "\n") str(study$best_params)

title: Getting Started with roptuna
text: Pruning stops unpromising trials early. Use trial$report() report intermediate values, check trial$should_prune(), call stop_prune() signal trial abandoned:
code: objective_with_pruning <- function(trial) {   lr <- trial$suggest_float("lr", 1e-5, 1e-1, log = TRUE)    for (epoch in 1:20) {     loss <- simulate_training(lr, epoch)  # your training function     trial$report(loss, step = epoch)     if (trial$should_prune()) stop_prune()   }   loss }  study <- create_study(   direction = "minimize",   sampler   = tpe_sampler(),   pruner    = median_pruner(n_startup_trials = 5) ) study$optimize(objective_with_pruning, n_trials = 30)

title: Getting Started with roptuna
text: Store trials file can resume later share colleagues:
code: study <- create_study(   direction  = "minimize",   storage    = sqlite_storage("my_study.sqlite"),   study_name = "quadratic" ) study$optimize(objective, n_trials = 20)  # Resume in a later session study2 <- create_study(   direction        = "minimize",   storage          = sqlite_storage("my_study.sqlite"),   study_name       = "quadratic",   load_if_exists   = TRUE ) study2$optimize(objective, n_trials = 20)  # adds 20 more trials

title: roptuna: A Practitioner's Guide to Hyperparameter Optimization
text: default sampler Tree-structured Parzen Estimator (TPE), introduced Bergstra et al. (2011). Understanding internally helps use well diagnose cases -performs.

title: roptuna: A Practitioner's Guide to Hyperparameter Optimization
text: configurable number startup trials (random exploration), TPE divides past trials two groups based objective value: Good trials (l group): best γ fraction completed trials (default γ = 0.25) Bad trials (g group): remaining 1 − γ fraction parameter, TPE fits kernel density estimate parameter values appeared group: ℓ(x)\ell(x) good trials g(x)g(x) bad trials. generates set candidate parameter values selects candidate expected improvement score ℓ(x)/g(x)\ell(x) / g(x) highest. intuition direct: region ℓ(x)\ell(x) high g(x)g(x) low region good trials concentrate bad trials — exactly next evaluation land.

title: roptuna: A Practitioner's Guide to Hyperparameter Optimization
text: scatter plot shows 20 past trials 1-D quadratic objective. Points coloured whether belong good (bottom 25%) bad (top 75%) group.  Given two groups, TPE fits kernel density estimate evaluates acquisition function ℓ(x)/g(x)\ell(x) / g(x) across domain. vertical dotted line marks ratio peaks — suggested location next trial.
code: set.seed(7) x_obs <- runif(20, -5, 5) y_obs <- x_obs^2 + rnorm(20, sd = 0.5)  gamma     <- 0.25 threshold <- quantile(y_obs, gamma) group     <- ifelse(y_obs <= threshold, "Good (bottom 25%)", "Bad (top 75%)")  ggplot(data.frame(x = x_obs, y = y_obs, group = group),        aes(x = x, y = y, colour = group, shape = group)) +   geom_point(size = 3.2, alpha = 0.85) +   geom_hline(yintercept = threshold, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey45") +   annotate("text", x = 4.0, y = threshold + 1.4,            label = sprintf("gamma threshold = %.1f", threshold),            colour = "grey40", size = 3.2) +   scale_colour_manual(values = c("Good (bottom 25%)" = "#2171b5",                                  "Bad (top 75%)"     = "#ef6548")) +   scale_shape_manual(values  = c("Good (bottom 25%)" = 16,                                  "Bad (top 75%)"     = 1)) +   labs(     title    = "TPE: classifying past trials into 'good' and 'bad'",     subtitle = "Good trials (bottom 25%) drive the density estimate l(x); bad trials drive g(x)",     x        = "Parameter value (x)", y = "Objective value f(x)",     colour   = NULL, shape = NULL   ) +   theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +   theme(legend.position = "bottom",         plot.title      = element_text(face = "bold"),         plot.subtitle   = element_text(colour = "grey40")) x_good <- x_obs[y_obs <= threshold] x_bad  <- x_obs[y_obs >  threshold] x_grid <- seq(-5, 5, length.out = 300) bw     <- 1.2  l_vals  <- sapply(x_grid, function(xi) mean(dnorm(xi, x_good, bw))) g_vals  <- sapply(x_grid, function(xi) mean(dnorm(xi, x_bad,  bw))) ei_vals <- l_vals / (g_vals + 1e-9)  l_norm  <- l_vals  / max(l_vals) g_norm  <- g_vals  / max(g_vals) ei_norm <- (ei_vals - min(ei_vals)) / diff(range(ei_vals)) next_x  <- x_grid[which.max(ei_vals)]  df_dens <- rbind(   data.frame(x = x_grid, y = l_norm,  curve = "l(x) — good trial density"),   data.frame(x = x_grid, y = g_norm,  curve = "g(x) — bad trial density"),   data.frame(x = x_grid, y = ei_norm, curve = "EI = l(x)/g(x) — acquisition") )  ggplot(df_dens, aes(x = x, y = y, colour = curve)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 0.9) +   geom_vline(xintercept = next_x, linetype = "dotted",              linewidth = 1.1, colour = "grey20") +   annotate("label", x = next_x + 0.3, y = 0.93,            label = sprintf("Next sample\nx = %.2f", next_x),            size = 3.1, fill = "white", label.size = 0.3, colour = "grey20") +   scale_colour_manual(values = c(     "l(x) — good trial density"     = "#2171b5",     "g(x) — bad trial density"      = "#ef6548",     "EI = l(x)/g(x) — acquisition"  = "#238b45"   )) +   labs(     title    = "TPE: density estimates and acquisition function",     subtitle = "Next trial is placed at the argmax of l(x)/g(x)",     x        = "Parameter value (x)", y = "Normalised value",     colour   = NULL   ) +   theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +   theme(legend.position = "bottom",         plot.title      = element_text(face = "bold"),         plot.subtitle   = element_text(colour = "grey40"))

title: roptuna: A Practitioner's Guide to Hyperparameter Optimization
text: Parameters idempotent within trial. Calling suggest_float("lr", 0, 1) twice trial always returns value. useful shared sub-function appears multiple times complex objective. Log-scale parameters used whenever quantity spans one order magnitude. Learning rates (10⁻⁵ 10⁻¹), regularisation penalties, kernel bandwidths common examples. linear scale, TPE’s Parzen estimate concentrates probability mass high end range; log scale distributes exploration evenly across magnitudes.
code:
study_demo <- create_study("minimize", sampler = tpe_sampler(seed = 7)) study_demo$optimize(function(trial) {   lr     <- trial$suggest_float("lr",     1e-5, 1e-1, log = TRUE)   depth  <- trial$suggest_int("depth",    2L, 8L)   method <- trial$suggest_categorical("method", c("adam", "sgd", "rmsprop"))   penalty <- c(adam = 0, sgd = 0.3, rmsprop = 0.6)[[method]]   (log10(1 / lr) - 3)^2 * 0.1 + 0.05 * depth + penalty }, n_trials = 40)  cat("Best method:", study_demo$best_params$method, "
") #> Best method: adam cat("Best lr:    ", formatC(study_demo$best_params$lr, format = "e", digits = 2), "
") #> Best lr:     9.95e-04 cat("Best depth: ", study_demo$best_params$depth, "
") #> Best depth:  2

title: roptuna: A Practitioner's Guide to Hyperparameter Optimization
text: practical advantage TPE random search visible good solutions occupy small fraction search space. 2-D bowl clear basin near (0, 0) TPE locates efficiently random search takes longer.
code: study_tpe <- create_study("minimize", sampler = tpe_sampler(seed = 123)) study_tpe$optimize(function(trial) {   x <- trial$suggest_float("x", -10, 10)   y <- trial$suggest_float("y", -10, 10)   x^2 + y^2 + 0.5 * x * y    # bowl with a ridge }, n_trials = 60)  study_rnd <- create_study("minimize", sampler = random_sampler(seed = 456)) study_rnd$optimize(function(trial) {   x <- trial$suggest_float("x", -10, 10)   y <- trial$suggest_float("y", -10, 10)   x^2 + y^2 + 0.5 * x * y }, n_trials = 60)  extract_history <- function(study, label) {   vals <- sapply(study$trials, `[[`, "value")   data.frame(trial = seq_along(vals), value = vals,              best = cummin(vals), sampler = label) }  hist_df <- rbind(   extract_history(study_tpe, "TPE"),   extract_history(study_rnd, "Random") )  ggplot(hist_df, aes(x = trial)) +   geom_point(aes(y = value, colour = sampler), alpha = 0.22, size = 1.3) +   geom_line(aes(y = best, colour = sampler), linewidth = 1.2) +   scale_colour_manual(values = c("TPE" = "#2171b5", "Random" = "#ef6548")) +   labs(     title    = "TPE vs Random: cumulative best on a 2-D bowl",     subtitle = "Lines show the running best; points show individual trial objective values",     x = "Trial number", y = "Objective value", colour = NULL   ) +   theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +   theme(legend.position = "bottom",         plot.title      = element_text(face = "bold"),         plot.subtitle   = element_text(colour = "grey40"))

title: roptuna: A Practitioner's Guide to Hyperparameter Optimization
text: grid sampler exhaustively evaluates every combination declared search space. appropriate space small complete coverage matters sample efficiency — example, ablating small set architectural choices.
code: study_grid <- create_study("minimize",   sampler = grid_sampler(list(     n_estimators = c(50L, 100L, 200L),     max_depth    = c(3L,  5L,   7L)   )) ) study_grid$optimize(function(trial) {   n <- trial$suggest_categorical("n_estimators", c(50L, 100L, 200L))   d <- trial$suggest_categorical("max_depth",    c(3L,  5L,  7L))   0.35 - 0.001 * as.numeric(n) + 0.01 * abs(as.numeric(d) - 4) }, n_trials = 9)  # 3 × 3 = 9 combinations  grid_df <- do.call(rbind, lapply(study_grid$trials, function(t)   data.frame(n_estimators = t$params$n_estimators,              max_depth    = t$params$max_depth,              value        = round(t$value, 4)))) print(grid_df[order(grid_df$value), ]) #>   n_estimators max_depth value #> 3          200         3  0.16 #> 6          200         5  0.16 #> 9          200         7  0.18 #> 2          100         3  0.26 #> 5          100         5  0.26 #> 8          100         7  0.28 #> 1           50         3  0.31 #> 4           50         5  0.31 #> 7           50         7  0.33

title: roptuna: A Practitioner's Guide to Hyperparameter Optimization
text: CMA-ES (Covariance Matrix Adaptation Evolution Strategy) sampler fits multivariate normal distribution completed trial parameter vectors adapts distribution’s mean, covariance matrix, step size every λ evaluations. concentrates search along principal axes objective landscape, makes particularly effective ill-conditioned continuous surfaces — elongated valleys, ridges, objectives parameters interact. CMA-ES applies suggest_float() parameters. Integer categorical parameters fall back independent sampling via TPE. global minimum Rosenbrock function 0 (1, 1). CMA-ES locates reliably covariance adaptation aligns sampling ellipsoid curved valley, whereas TPE random search require far trials probe narrow optimal region.
code:
study_cmaes <- create_study("minimize",   sampler = cmaes_sampler(n_startup_trials = 10L, seed = 42L) ) study_cmaes$optimize(function(trial) {   x <- trial$suggest_float("x", -5, 5)   y <- trial$suggest_float("y", -5, 5)   # Rosenbrock function: narrow curved valley; notoriously hard for axis-aligned methods   100 * (y - x^2)^2 + (1 - x)^2 }, n_trials = 100)  cat("Best value:", round(study_cmaes$best_value, 4), "
") #> Best value: 0.0566 cat("Best x:    ", round(study_cmaes$best_params$x, 4), "
") #> Best x:     0.7696 cat("Best y:    ", round(study_cmaes$best_params$y, 4), "
") #> Best y:     0.5863

title: roptuna: A Practitioner's Guide to Hyperparameter Optimization
text: Pruning terminates unpromising trials early, consume full evaluation budget. trial whose intermediate performance clearly worse existing trials can abandoned fraction epochs, freeing compute promising candidates.

title: roptuna: A Practitioner's Guide to Hyperparameter Optimization
text: objective function reports intermediate values trial$report(), checks trial$should_prune(), signals early termination stop_prune().
code: study_prune <- create_study(   "minimize",   sampler = tpe_sampler(seed = 42),   pruner  = median_pruner(n_startup_trials = 5L, n_warmup_steps = 3L) )  study_prune$optimize(function(trial) {   lr <- trial$suggest_float("lr", 1e-4, 1e-1, log = TRUE)   for (epoch in seq_len(20)) {     loss <- exp(-lr * epoch) + 0.1 + rnorm(1, 0, 0.03)     trial$report(loss, step = as.integer(epoch))     if (trial$should_prune()) stop_prune()   }   loss }, n_trials = 40)  cat("Trial state distribution:\n") #> Trial state distribution: print(table(sapply(study_prune$trials, `[[`, "state"))) #>  #> complete   pruned  #>       11       29

title: roptuna: A Practitioner's Guide to Hyperparameter Optimization
text: Training curves trials reveal pruner’s behaviour: trials high loss early (low learning rate, slow convergence) cut short, trials converge quickly (high learning rate) run completion.
code: traces <- Filter(Negate(is.null), lapply(study_prune$trials, function(t) {   iv <- t$intermediate_values   if (length(iv) == 0) return(NULL)   data.frame(     trial  = t$number,     epoch  = as.integer(names(iv)),     loss   = unlist(iv),     state  = t$state,     stringsAsFactors = FALSE   ) })) trace_df <- do.call(rbind, traces) trace_df$label <- ifelse(trace_df$state == "pruned", "Pruned", "Completed")  ggplot(trace_df, aes(x = epoch, y = loss,                      group = trial, colour = label)) +   geom_line(alpha = 0.55, linewidth = 0.65) +   scale_colour_manual(values = c("Pruned" = "#ef6548", "Completed" = "#2171b5")) +   labs(     title    = "Trial training curves with MedianPruner",     subtitle = "Pruned trials (red) are stopped when their loss exceeds the running median",     x        = "Training epoch", y = "Loss", colour = NULL   ) +   theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +   theme(legend.position = "bottom",         plot.title      = element_text(face = "bold"),         plot.subtitle   = element_text(colour = "grey40"))

title: roptuna: A Practitioner's Guide to Hyperparameter Optimization
text: Hyperband (Li et al., 2017) removes sensitivity Successive Halving choice min_resource running multiple SHA brackets parallel. new trial assigned bracket trial index (round-robin), brackets operate different resource rungs simultaneously without requiring pick one configuration upfront.
code: study_hb <- create_study(   "minimize",   sampler = tpe_sampler(seed = 42),   pruner  = hyperband_pruner(     min_resource            = 1L,     reduction_factor        = 3L,     min_early_stopping_rate = 0L,     n_brackets              = 3L   ) ) study_hb$optimize(function(trial) {   lr <- trial$suggest_float("lr", 1e-4, 1e-1, log = TRUE)   for (epoch in seq_len(27)) {     loss <- exp(-lr * epoch) + 0.1 + rnorm(1, 0, 0.02)     trial$report(loss, step = as.integer(epoch))     if (trial$should_prune()) stop_prune()   }   loss }, n_trials = 40)  cat("State distribution:\n") #> State distribution: print(table(sapply(study_hb$trials, `[[`, "state"))) #>  #> complete   pruned  #>        3       37

title: roptuna: A Practitioner's Guide to Hyperparameter Optimization
text: WilcoxonPruner applies one-sided Wilcoxon rank-sum test step, comparing current trial’s loss completed trials step. trial pruned p-value falls p_threshold, indicating high statistical confidence current trial’s loss distribution stochastically worse existing trials. pruner principled fixed-threshold rule adapts automatically scale objective values.
code: study_wx <- create_study(   "minimize",   sampler = tpe_sampler(seed = 42),   pruner  = wilcoxon_pruner(p_threshold = 0.1, n_startup_trials = 5L) ) study_wx$optimize(function(trial) {   lr <- trial$suggest_float("lr", 1e-4, 1e-1, log = TRUE)   for (epoch in seq_len(20)) {     loss <- exp(-lr * epoch) + 0.1 + rnorm(1, 0, 0.03)     trial$report(loss, step = as.integer(epoch))     if (trial$should_prune()) stop_prune()   }   loss }, n_trials = 40)  cat("State distribution:\n") #> State distribution: print(table(sapply(study_wx$trials, `[[`, "state"))) #>  #> complete   pruned  #>       32        8

title: roptuna: A Practitioner's Guide to Hyperparameter Optimization
text: Many real problems require balancing competing objectives simultaneously — maximising model accuracy minimising inference latency, maximising R² minimising model complexity. Single-objective optimisation collapses trade-scalar, requires commit weighting search begins. Multi-objective optimisation instead explores full Pareto front: set non-dominated solutions objective can improved without worsening another.

title: roptuna: A Practitioner's Guide to Hyperparameter Optimization
text: Pass character vector directions= instead single string. Use nsgaii_sampler(), maintains population diversity along front via non-dominated sorting crowding distance selection. objective function returns numeric vector — one element per direction. study$best_trials returns non-dominated subset.
code: study_mo <- create_study(   directions = c("minimize", "minimize"),   sampler    = nsgaii_sampler(population_size = 30L, seed = 5L) )  study_mo$optimize(function(trial) {   x <- trial$suggest_float("x", 0, 1)   y <- trial$suggest_float("y", 0, 1)   # Objective 1: distance from (0, 0)  — prefers lower-left   # Objective 2: distance from (1, 1)  — prefers upper-right   c(x^2 + y^2, (x - 1)^2 + (y - 1)^2) }, n_trials = 90)  cat("Total trials:          ", study_mo$n_trials, "\n") #> Total trials:           90 cat("Pareto-optimal trials: ", length(study_mo$best_trials), "\n") #> Pareto-optimal trials:  27

title: roptuna: A Practitioner's Guide to Hyperparameter Optimization
text: standard optimize() loop samples parameters evaluates objective single synchronous cycle. ask() / tell() API decouples two steps, giving explicit control trials created results recorded. useful : objective evaluated external system (remote API, subprocess, hardware device) operates asynchronously. need inspect transform sampled parameters submitting evaluator. want run batched external evaluations report results bulk.

title: roptuna: A Practitioner's Guide to Hyperparameter Optimization
code: study_at <- create_study("minimize", sampler = tpe_sampler(seed = 42L))  for (i in seq_len(30)) {   trial <- study_at$ask()                          # create trial, sample params   x     <- trial$suggest_float("x", -5, 5)         # read sampled parameter   value <- x^2 + rnorm(1, 0, 0.1)                  # evaluate (could be external)   study_at$tell(trial, value)                       # record result }  cat("Best value:", round(study_at$best_value, 4), "\n") #> Best value: -0.0994 cat("Best x:    ", round(study_at$best_params$x, 4), "\n") #> Best x:     -0.2143

text: systems accept batch inputs return results asynchronously, can keep multiple open trials simultaneously: tell() accepts optional state argument ("complete" default; also "pruned" "failed") cases evaluation cut short failed.
code: study_batch <- create_study("minimize")  # Sample 4 parameter sets at once open_trials <- lapply(seq_len(4), function(i) study_batch$ask()) params      <- lapply(open_trials, function(t) t$suggest_float("x", -5, 5))  # ... send params to external evaluator; wait for results ... values <- lapply(params, function(x) x^2)  # placeholder: local evaluation  # Record results (order does not matter) for (i in seq_along(open_trials)) {   study_batch$tell(open_trials[[i]], values[[i]]) }

title: roptuna: A Practitioner's Guide to Hyperparameter Optimization
text: study$optimize() accepts n_jobs argument distributes trial evaluations across multiple R worker processes using parallel package. reduces wall-clock time trial takes seconds minutes machine multiple cores available.
code: study_par <- create_study("minimize", sampler = random_sampler(seed = 1L)) study_par$optimize(   function(trial) {     x <- trial$suggest_float("x", -5, 5)     y <- trial$suggest_float("y", -5, 5)     x^2 + y^2   },   n_trials = 20L,   n_jobs   = 2L ) cat("Completed trials:", study_par$n_trials, "\n") #> Completed trials: 20 cat("Best value:      ", round(study_par$best_value, 4), "\n") #> Best value:       0.8434

title: roptuna: A Practitioner's Guide to Hyperparameter Optimization
text: main process samples parameter values batch n_jobs trials using configured sampler. Parameter sets serialised dispatched PSOCK worker processes via parallel package. Workers evaluate objective function return objective values. main process records results storage repeats n_trials exhausted. storage writes happen main process, parallel execution safe InMemoryStorage SqliteStorage without additional configuration.

title: roptuna: A Practitioner's Guide to Hyperparameter Optimization
text: SQLite persistence provides durable, queryable record survives session restarts can shared across machines. load_if_exists = TRUE flag finds existing study name database rather creating fresh one. prior trials immediately available study$trials, new sampler uses inform subsequent suggestions — optimisation continues previous session left .
code:
tmp_db <- tempfile(fileext = ".sqlite")  # First session: run 20 trials study_s1 <- create_study(   "minimize",   sampler    = tpe_sampler(seed = 11),   storage    = sqlite_storage(tmp_db),   study_name = "persistent-demo" ) study_s1$optimize(function(trial) trial$suggest_float("x", -5, 5)^2,                   n_trials = 20) cat("Session 1 best:", round(study_s1$best_value, 5), "
") #> Session 1 best: 0.01125  # Second session: reload and add 20 more trials study_s2 <- create_study(   "minimize",   sampler        = tpe_sampler(seed = 22),   storage        = sqlite_storage(tmp_db),   study_name     = "persistent-demo",   load_if_exists = TRUE ) study_s2$optimize(function(trial) trial$suggest_float("x", -5, 5)^2,                   n_trials = 20) cat("Session 2 total trials:", length(study_s2$trials), "
") #> Session 2 total trials: 40 cat("Overall best:          ", round(study_s2$best_value, 6), "
") #> Overall best:           0.00037  unlink(tmp_db)

what: A Realistic Example: Tuning a Decision Tree on Iris
title: roptuna: A Practitioner's Guide to Hyperparameter Optimization
text: section demonstrates complete HPO workflow real dataset: three-hyperparameter search including log-scale parameter, 5-fold cross-validation objective, three plot types applied results.    importance plot reveals three hyperparameters influences cross-validated accuracy. iris, cp (complexity parameter sets minimum improvement required split) typically dominates — tree structure sensitive -splitting maximum depth alone, iris nearly linearly separable shallow trees suffice.
code: set.seed(42)  iris_objective <- function(trial) {   minsplit <- trial$suggest_int("minsplit", 2L, 40L)   maxdepth <- trial$suggest_int("maxdepth", 1L, 12L)   cp       <- trial$suggest_float("cp", 0.0001, 0.5, log = TRUE)    folds <- sample(rep(1:5, length.out = nrow(iris)))   acc <- vapply(1:5, function(k) {     tr  <- iris[folds != k, ]     te  <- iris[folds == k, ]     fit <- rpart::rpart(       Species ~ ., data = tr, method = "class",       control = rpart::rpart.control(         minsplit = minsplit, maxdepth = maxdepth, cp = cp       )     )     mean(predict(fit, te, type = "class") == te$Species)   }, numeric(1))   1 - mean(acc) }  study_iris <- create_study("minimize", sampler = tpe_sampler(seed = 42)) study_iris$optimize(iris_objective, n_trials = 60)  cat("Best CV error rate:", round(study_iris$best_value, 4), "\n") #> Best CV error rate: 0.04 cat("Best params:\n"); str(study_iris$best_params) #> Best params: #> List of 3 #>  $ minsplit: int 8 #>  $ maxdepth: int 5 #>  $ cp      : num 0.00254 autoplot(study_iris, type = "history") +   labs(subtitle = "iris CART: 5-fold CV error over 60 trials") +   theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +   theme(plot.title    = element_text(face = "bold"),         plot.subtitle = element_text(colour = "grey40")) autoplot(study_iris, type = "parallel_coordinate") +   theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +   theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold")) autoplot(study_iris, type = "param_importance") +   theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +   theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))

title: roptuna: A Practitioner's Guide to Hyperparameter Optimization
text: Allow roughly 10–20 trials per active hyperparameter TPE move past startup phase build meaningful surface model. three hyperparameters, 50–100 trials reasonable starting budget. objective cheap (second per trial), run 200 ; improvement sample efficiency compounds. expensive objectives (minutes per trial), consider starting random search build baseline, switching TPE.

title: roptuna: A Practitioner's Guide to Hyperparameter Optimization
text: n_startup_trials argument (default 10) controls many random trials run TPE activates. Increasing reduces risk overfitting unlucky initial sample; decreasing starts Bayesian exploitation earlier thinner evidence. fewer 3 active parameters, n_startup_trials = 5 usually sufficient. 10 parameters, consider n_startup_trials = 20.

title: roptuna: A Practitioner's Guide to Hyperparameter Optimization
text: roptuna’s define--run design handles conditional parameters naturally — call suggest_*() parameters active given earlier choices. Parameters never suggested best trial simply appear best_params. expected behaviour conditional spaces requires special handling.
code:
study_cond <- create_study("minimize", sampler = tpe_sampler(seed = 42)) study_cond$optimize(function(trial) {   model <- trial$suggest_categorical("model", c("linear", "tree"))   if (model == "linear") {     alpha <- trial$suggest_float("alpha", 0.0001, 10, log = TRUE)     0.5 + log10(alpha + 1) * 0.08   } else {     depth <- trial$suggest_int("depth", 1L, 8L)     0.3 + 0.05 * depth   } }, n_trials = 40)  cat("Best model:", study_cond$best_params$model, "
") #> Best model: tree if (study_cond$best_params$model == "tree") {   cat("Best depth:", study_cond$best_params$depth, "
") } else {   cat("Best alpha:", round(study_cond$best_params$alpha, 4), "
") } #> Best depth: 1

title: roptuna: A Practitioner's Guide to Hyperparameter Optimization
text: objective occasionally fails — NaN loss, numerical overflow, --memory error extreme hyperparameter combination — use catch argument absorb specific error classes rather aborting study: Failed trials stored state "failed" excluded TPE’s model. study continues without interruption, failed trial count gives signal extreme region search space problematic.
code: study_robust <- create_study("minimize") study_robust$optimize(   function(trial) {     x <- trial$suggest_float("x", -5, 5)     if (x > 4.2) stop("simulated domain error")     x^2   },   n_trials = 25,   catch    = "simpleError" ) cat("State distribution:\n") #> State distribution: print(table(sapply(study_robust$trials, `[[`, "state"))) #>  #> complete   failed  #>       22        3

title: roptuna: A Practitioner's Guide to Hyperparameter Optimization
text: production HPO wall-clock time matters fixed trial count, use timeout (seconds) instead addition n_trials: loop stops end first trial completes timeout — trial interrupted mid-evaluation.
code: study$optimize(objective, n_trials = 1000L, timeout = 3600)  # stop after 1 hour

code: tune_optuna(   object,   resamples,   suggest_fn,   n_trials = 20,   direction = "minimize",   sampler = tpe_sampler(),   pruner = NULL,   storage = NULL,   study_name = NULL,   metrics = NULL,   control = NULL )

title: Run a tidymodels workflow using roptuna for hyperparameter search — tune_optuna
text: object workflow object tune::tune() placeholders. resamples rset object (e.g. rsample::vfold_cv()). suggest_fn function function(trial) -> named list mapping trial suggestions parameter names. Parameter names must match workflow's tuning parameters. n_trials Number trials run. direction "minimize" "maximize". sampler roptuna sampler. Defaults tpe_sampler(). pruner roptuna pruner NULL. storage roptuna storage backend NULL (-memory). study_name Character study name. metrics yardstick::metric_set. Defaults workflow's default metric. control tune::control_resamples() object.

title: Run a tidymodels workflow using roptuna for hyperparameter search — tune_optuna
text: roptuna Study object. Use study$best_params retrieve best hyperparameters.

title: Run a tidymodels workflow using roptuna for hyperparameter search — tune_optuna
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Requires tune, parsnip, rsample, workflows, yardstick packages library(tune); library(parsnip); library(rsample); library(workflows) wf <- workflow() |>   add_model(decision_tree(cost_complexity = tune()) |> set_engine("rpart") |>               set_mode("classification")) |>   add_formula(Species ~ .) folds <- vfold_cv(iris, v = 3) study <- tune_optuna(wf, folds,   suggest_fn = function(trial) list(cost_complexity = trial$suggest_float("cp", 0, 0.1)),   n_trials = 5, direction = "minimize") study$best_params } # }
